name: Mark Weiser's Ubiquitous Computing Vision
short_description: Mark Weiser's vision of computing that recedes into the environment as 'calm technology'—embedded, ambient, and so deeply woven into everyday life that it effectively disappears.
long_description: Articulated by Mark Weiser at Xerox PARC, most famously in 'The Computer for the 21st Century' (Scientific American, 1991), ubiquitous computing imagines hundreds of networked computers per person embedded throughout rooms, objects, and surfaces rather than concentrated in a single PC. Weiser proposed a hierarchy of inch-, foot-, and yard-scale devices (tabs, pads, and boards) and, with John Seely Brown, the principle of 'calm technology' that moves fluidly between the center and periphery of attention, informing without demanding it. The vision—that 'the most profound technologies are those that disappear'—became the conceptual ancestor of ambient intelligence, the Internet of Things, smart homes, and much of today's physical and embedded AI.

images.caption: Mark Weiser, chief technologist at Xerox PARC and originator of the ubiquitous computing vision.
images.alt_text: Portrait photograph of Mark Weiser, a man with short dark hair and glasses, looking toward the camera.

sources.title: The Computer for the 21st Century
sources.access_notes: Vol. 265, No. 3 (September 1991). The foundational essay introducing ubiquitous computing and the tabs/pads/boards device hierarchy.

sources.title: Mark Weiser
sources.access_notes: Biography: 1952–1999, CTO at Xerox PARC, coined 'ubiquitous computing' in 1988, co-author of 'Designing Calm Technology' with John Seely Brown.

sources.title: Designing Calm Technology / Calm Technology principles
sources.access_notes: Archive and discussion of Weiser & Brown's calm-technology framing of the periphery and center of attention.

interactions.title: A blank tab beeps with a colleague's name
interactions.description: In Weiser's 'Sal' scenario, an inch-scale tab on the desk lights up showing 'Joe'; she picks it up, gestures with it toward the wall-sized liveboard, and Joe's shared document and voice appear—computing summoned by a flick rather than a login.

interactions.title: Tabs, pads, and boards as one continuous workspace
interactions.description: Weiser proposed hundreds of devices at three scales—inch-sized tabs like active Post-its, foot-sized pads handled like loose sheets of paper, and yard-sized boards used as shared blackboards—so that a person picks up whichever surface is at hand and the work follows them around the room.

interactions.title: The room knows where you are
interactions.description: The PARCTab, built by Roy Want's team as the testbed for the vision, used infrared to report a person's location to the building, so doors, displays, and call-forwarding could react to who walked in—an early context-aware computer descended from the Olivetti Active Badge.

interactions.title: Network traffic you feel rather than read
interactions.description: Weiser and Brown pointed to Natalie Jeremijenko's 'Dangling String'—an 8-foot plastic cord on a ceiling motor wired to the Ethernet—as calm technology: a busy network set it whirling and noisy, a quiet one let it twitch, putting data in the periphery where you notice it without looking.

interactions.title: The vision shipped as the smart home
interactions.description: Decades on, the everyday realization is unglamorous and exactly as predicted: networked thermostats, speakers, locks, and sensors scattered through a house, coordinating in the background—the ambient-intelligence and Internet-of-Things lineage Weiser's essay seeded.

interactions.title: Hand the wall a document from your pocket
interactions.description: Grounded in the tab-to-board gesture Weiser described, a small personal device could fling whatever you're holding onto the nearest shared display the moment you point at it—no pairing dialog, just the room recognizing intent.

interactions.title: Presence that follows you between rooms
interactions.description: Building on the location and environmental sensing at the heart of the vision, the music, lighting, and the call you're on could quietly migrate to whatever screen and speaker you've walked toward, the environment treating the whole building as one device.

interactions.title: An ambient dial for things that don't deserve a screen
interactions.description: Following the Dangling String's logic, a slow physical indicator—a tilting surface, a warming object—could carry one low-stakes signal like air quality or a far-off family member's wakefulness in your periphery, informing without ever demanding a glance.
